journal-title-group.journal-title: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation

title-group.article-title: Comparative analysis of body composition and pulmonary function in elite male deaf endurance athletes – track and field and road cycling

abstract.p: The physiological determinants of respiratory function in elite athletes with hearing impairment remain unexplored. It is unknown whether sport-specific adaptations or the unique neurophysiology of deafness primarily govern their pulmonary performance, and if standard spirometric criteria are applicable. This study aimed to conduct the first comprehensive multivariate characterization of anthropometric and training-related predictors of pulmonary function in elite deaf athletes, comparing disciplines with contrasting physiological demands. Thirty-three elite male deaf athletes (track-and-field, n = 12; road cycling, n = 21) underwent rigorous spirometry and body composition analysis. We employed bivariate correlations, multiple linear regression, a novel multivariate Euclidean distance analysis, and a test-retest reliability assessment to identify independent predictors of key respiratory parameters and quantify overall physiological distinctness. We identified a unique spirometric signature in deaf athletes, characterized by abbreviated forced expiration times (FET100% < 6s) and elevated extrapolated volume (VEXT), suggesting a neurophysiological adaptation in respiratory motor control due to absent auditory feedback. Multivariate analysis confirmed distinct, sport-specific physiological profiles (between-group Euclidean distance: 6.91). The determinants of lung function were fundamentally different: in track athletes, Fat-Free Mass was the dominant predictor of FEV1 (β = 0.95, R² = 0.92, p = 0.002), reflecting strength-oriented adaptations. In cyclists, body height was the primary determinant (β = 0.71, R² = 0.86, p < 0.001), indicating a structural influence. Body height exerted opposing effects on MEF25% between sports (track: β = − 1.45; cycling: β = 0.81), further underscoring discipline-specific pathways. Track athletes also demonstrated superior technical proficiency in spirometry execution (83.3% “Excellent” maneuvers vs. 66.7% in cyclists). Crucially, an analysis of measurement reliability quantitatively corroborated this difference through superior test-retest reliability metrics: they showed a lower Minimal Detectable Change for FEV1 (MDC95 = 0.11 L vs. 0.31 L in cyclists), while primary outcomes (FVC, FEV1) demonstrated excellent reliability (ICC > 0.90) in both groups. The respiratory physiology of elite deaf athletes is not defined by auditory deficit but by sport-specific adaptation and a unique respiratory neurology. Our findings challenge the applicability of standard pulmonary models and criteria to this population. Coaches and clinicians are recommended to adopt discipline-specific frameworks and tailored communication for respiratory assessment and performance optimization in deaf athletes, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s13102-026-01619-7.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Evidence regarding the impact of regular sports training on spirometric variables in individuals with hearing loss remains limited, and it is unclear whether the breathing patterns of elite deaf athletes conform to the standards established by leading respiratory societies, such as the American Thoracic Society and the European Respiratory Society [1]. Previous research suggests that children and adolescents with congenital or acquired hearing loss may experience respiratory dysfunction due to overlapping mechanisms, including impaired coordination between speech and breathing. Because auditory feedback is incomplete, children with hearing impairment struggle to monitor their own vocal output, which hinders the development of proper phonation. As a result, they may demonstrate atypical breathing patterns, including shortened expiratory phases, inefficient air usage, and excessive activation of respiratory muscles, leading to reduced vital capacity [2–4]. Although auditory verbal therapy can improve voice quality, acoustic parameters often remain abnormal compared with peers who have normal hearing. Spirometry in children with hearing loss frequently reveals decreased vital capacity (VC), reduced muscle strength, and lower flow parameters such as peak expiratory flow (PEF) and forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1), deficits thought to result partly from diminished vocal stimulation and lower levels of physical activity [5].

Żebrowska reported that children and adolescents aged 10–16 years with hearing loss had lower spirometric values–including VC, FEV1, PEF, FEF25–75%, and maximal voluntary ventilation (MVV) – than hearing controls, with the largest deficits observed in PEF and MVV, highlighting the adverse impact of sensory deprivation on respiratory function [6]. Similarly, Jonsson suggested that reduced speech activity may negatively influence respiratory development, as deaf children exhibited significantly lower median VC and FEV1 values than their hearing peers, and even the most physically active failed to achieve values comparable to hearing controls [7]. Congenital hearing loss also exerts a broader influence on development, often accompanied by vestibular dysfunction affecting spatial orientation and balance [8]. These overlapping sensory deficits may influence the development of complex motor programs, including respiratory control patterns, although compensatory mechanisms involving visual and sensory integration can enable children with hearing loss to achieve motor development levels comparable to healthy peers [9–11]. Additionally, reduced physical activity relative to hearing peers may further compromise respiratory efficiency [12–17].

Regular physical exercise plays a key role in the rehabilitation of deaf children and adolescents, improving balance, coordination, respiratory endurance, and overall motor performance [18–24]. Systematic reviews have demonstrated that structured sports participation can effectively improve motor performance and physiological function in this population [25]. Szulc’s studies on deaf football players revealed significant differences in motor performance between athletes and inactive deaf individuals [26], while other publications report positive effects of sport training on motor abilities in deaf athletes [27–36]. For instance, Szulc and Sandurska documented differences in the sports performance of deaf and hearing swimmers participating in the Deaflympics and the Olympic Games [37]. While numerous publications exist on the ventilatory capacity of able-bodied competitive athletes compared with sedentary groups [38–45], research on deaf athletes remains scarce.

Critically, analyzing somatic or ventilatory characteristics in isolation provides only a partial understanding of functional potential in deaf athletes. Body composition directly influences biomechanics, energy expenditure, and muscle power, while spirometric indices determine ventilatory efficiency and aerobic capacity. Integrating these evaluations is therefore essential for identifying discipline-specific adaptations, detecting possible functional limitations, and designing comprehensive training strategies that combine nutritional optimization, body composition monitoring, and respiratory coaching. Such an approach also allows for more precise comparisons with hearing athletes, providing new insights into how hearing loss shapes both physical development and ventilatory function. Dauty and colleagues emphasize that spirometric reference values derived from non-athletic populations should not be directly applied to elite athletes, as long-term training induces sport-specific adaptations [40].

In deaf individuals, most spirometric investigations have focused on standard parameters such as forced vital capacity (FVC), FEV1, FEV1/FVC ratio, and PEF, while less attention has been given to mid-expiratory flows (FEF25–75%) or qualitative indices of test performance, including flow-volume curve morphology. According to ATS/ERS guidelines [1], reliable spirometry requires at least two FVC and two FEV1 values within 150 mL of each other. While this standard has been applied in studies of deaf individuals, underlying respiratory differences may still complicate interpretation, and potential abnormalities in flow-volume curves–such as submaximal effort or premature termination–are often overlooked. Ivan’s work is one of the few to include qualitative analysis of spirometric curves, revealing distinctive deviations in morphology [46]. Another underexplored domain is maximal oxygen uptake (VO2max) assessment in deaf athletes. Baranauskas et al. [33] investigated VO2max in elite deaf female basketball players from the Lithuanian national team, reporting an average aerobic capacity of 55.9 mL·min⁻¹·kg⁻¹. The authors suggest this may result from restricted lung volume development due to reduced speaking, singing, or shouting activity, while noting that values comparable to healthy athletes indicate that appropriate training can improve respiratory function in this group.

The relatively small number of studies highlights the need for research integrating body composition analysis with advanced spirometric and performance measures in elite deaf athletes. Despite consistent evidence of impaired respiratory function in individuals with hearing loss, little is known about whether systematic, high-level training can mitigate these disadvantages and normalize pulmonary performance. Previous work has primarily focused on children and adolescents, with limited attention to elite deaf athletes, and even fewer studies have combined body composition analyses with advanced spirometric indices. Moreover, applying reference values derived from general populations to athletes risks misinterpretation of respiratory efficiency in this unique group.

Therefore, the aim of this study was to conduct a comparative analysis of body composition and pulmonary function in elite deaf endurance athletes, specifically track-and-field runners and road cyclists. By integrating anthropometric and spirometric measurements, we sought to determine whether regular high-intensity training across endurance disciplines can compensate for reported deficits in respiratory function and establish reference values tailored to this athletic population. Based on prior evidence, we formulated the following hypotheses: H1: track-and-field athletes would demonstrate higher spirometric values and more homogeneous ventilatory performance than road cyclists, reflecting sport-specific respiratory adaptations. H2: Deaf athletes would demonstrate lower FVC and FEV1 values compared with references reported for hearing endurance athletes, indicating persistent functional limitations despite systematic training.

sec.title: Study population and eligibility criteria
sec.p: The study cohort comprised elite male athletes with hearing impairment. All participants met the International Committee of Sports for the Deaf requirement of a documented hearing loss exceeding 55 dB in the better ear—the minimum threshold for competition in deaf sports. As shown in Table 1, the majority of athletes in both groups presented with severe-to-profound hearing loss, consistent with the elite deaf athlete population.

caption.p: Degree of hearing loss in track-and-field (ATH) and cycling (CYCL) participants, according to WHO classification [47] (average of both ears)

table-wrap-foot.p: Hearing loss was classified using standard WHO criteria based on the pure-tone average (PTA) in the better ear: Normal Hearing (< 20 dB), Mild (20–34 dB), Moderate (35–49 dB), Moderately severe (50–64 dB), Severe (65–79 dB), Profound (80–94 dB), Deafness (≥ 95 dB) [47]. Thresholds represent the mean of left and right ears

sec.p: Participants were recruited from the highest competitive tiers of their sports: track and field (n = 12) and road cycling (n = 21). To ensure sample homogeneity, strict eligibility criteria were applied:

list-item.p: Current participation in international-level competitions.
A minimum training volume of ≥ 10 h per week.
No history of tobacco use.
No current medication use.
Absence of comorbidities that could affect pulmonary function or body composition.

sec.p: For the cyclists, an additional criterion required representation on European national teams to control for geographical and training background factors.

Given the rarity of the population, a convenience sampling approach was used, encompassing all eligible elite deaf endurance athletes available during the study period. A post-hoc sensitivity analysis was conducted to quantify statistical power and the minimum detectable effect size; these results are discussed in the Limitations section.

sec.title: Recruitment and data collection
sec.p: Data collection was conducted during two major sporting events: the Polish National Deaf Athletics Team training camp (encompassing middle- and long-distance running specialties) and the 2024 World Deaf Road Cycling Championships held in Poland. To maintain methodological consistency, all measurements were performed by a single investigator using standardized protocols and calibrated equipment. The international cycling cohort represented six European nations with the following distribution: Poland (27.3%), Germany (18.2%), with France, Ukraine, Greece, and Portugal each contributing 9.1% of participants. The multinational composition of the cycling cohort may improve the representativeness of our findings for European elite deaf cyclists.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: This study was approved by the Bioethics Committee of the Collegium Medicum, Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń, Poland (Approval No. KB 236/2023). Written informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to enrollment. For athletes younger than 18 years, consent was additionally obtained from their legal guardians and coaches. All participants or their guardians were fully informed about the study procedures before providing written consent. The research was conducted in accordance with the ethical principles of the Declaration of Helsinki.

sec.title: Communication and study description
sec.p: Prior to testing, all participants received a detailed briefing that included a written description of the study procedures in both Polish and English. Communication was additionally supported through sign language, with assistance provided by a coach or a certified interpreter from the national team. To ensure full comprehension and proper execution of the protocols, a trial session was conducted. During this session, potential difficulties were identified, and the correct techniques for performing the measurements were demonstrated and clarified.

sec.title: Survey study
sec.p: Data on hearing status, training history, and competitive experience were collected using a structured questionnaire a self-administered paper-based questionnaire [48] (see: Supplementary materials 2). The instrument included the following items:

list-item.p: Degree of hearing loss in the left and right ear (dB),
Duration of training (years),
Training together with hearing athletes (yes/no),
Frequency of training sessions per week,
Average daily training duration (hours),
Number of participations in the Deaflympics Games.

sec.title: Body composition assessment
sec.p: Body Height measurement (BH [cm]) was performed using the InBody BSM 170B stadiometer (South Korea). Body weight and segmental body composition analysis were conducted with the Tanita BC 418 MA body composition analyzer (Japan), equipped with 8 electrodes [49, 50]. Accuracy 0.1 kg, factory calibration, up to 300,000 measurements without the need for recalibration. The assessment included the following parameters:

list-item.p: BM – Body mass [kg],
BMI [kg/m²] – Body mass index.
BMR – Basal metabolic rate [kcal/day],
FAT% – Percentage of body fat [%],
FAT – Fat mass [kg],
FFM – Fat-free mass [kg],
TBW – Total body water content [kg],
RL-FAT; LL-FAT – Fat mass in the right or left lower leg [kg],
RL-FFM; LL-FFM – Fat-free mass in the right or left lower leg [kg],
TRUNK FAT% – Percentage of fat in the trunk [%],
TRUNK FAT MASS – Fat mass in the trunk [kg],
TRUNK FFM – Fat-free mass in the trunk [kg].

sec.title: Spirometry examination
sec.p: Spirometry was conducted using a MicroQuark spirometer with Omnia software (Cosmed, Italy) in strict accordance with American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society (ATS/ERS) guidelines [1]. The device was equipped with a bidirectional digital turbine flowmeter (flow range: 0–16 L/s; accuracy: ±2%; internal resistance: < 0.8 cmH₂O·L⁻¹·s at ~ 14 L/s). A 3-liter calibration pump was used for daily calibration.

All assessments were performed in an air-conditioned laboratory under standardized conditions (ambient temperature: 18–22 °C). Participants were seated, wore a nose clip, and held the spirometer. After receiving standardized instructions through combined verbal explanation and visual demonstration, each athlete was instructed to seal their lips around a disposable mouthpiece, place the tongue beneath it, and perform a series of maneuvers. Following a period of quiet tidal breathing, each maneuver consisted of a maximal inhalation, immediately followed by a rapid, forceful, and sustained exhalation lasting at least six seconds to ensure complete measurement of all parameters, including FEV1.

A maximum of six attempts were allowed to obtain at least two technically acceptable and reproducible maneuvers per ATS/ERS criteria, with the limit set to prevent fatigue–particularly relevant for participants with hearing impairment who might require more guidance. Consecutive maneuvers were separated by a 10–15 min rest period, an interval determined sufficient for elite athletes to ensure full recovery and reproducible peak performance without respiratory muscle fatigue.

The spirometer’s integrated software provided a qualitative rating for each maneuver (e.g., “Excellent,” “Good,” “Satisfactory,” “Barely Passing”) based on predefined volume repeatability criteria (e.g., “Excellent” ≤ 0.15 L for FEV1 and FVC). For the analysis of within-session test-retest reliability, the two highest-quality maneuvers from each participant were selected.
The analysis included the following parameters:

list-item.p: FVC [L] – Forced Vital Capacity - the maximum amount of air that a person can expel from the lungs during a maximum forced and rapid exhalation after a complete, maximum inhalation.
FEV1 [L] – Forced Expiratory Volume in one second - the volume of air that the patient is able to exhale from the lungs during the first second of a maximum forced exhalation.

FEV1/FVC [%] – Ratio of FEV1 to FVC expressed as a percentage. Correct value only with reliable FVC and FEV1; sensitive to technical errors. Difference between the best samples ≤ 0.150 L – a condition for considering the test reliable.
PEF [L/s] – Peak Expiratory Flow. An indicator of the initial force of expiration. A very low PEF may indicate poor expiratory muscle engagement or insufficient patient effort.

FEF25–75% [L/s] – Mean expiratory flow between 25% and 75% of FVC. Evaluation of flow in the mid-expiratory phase. This parameter is underestimated during incomplete exhalation, making it a marker of exhalation quality.
FET100% [s] – Forced Expiratory Time. Expiratory duration assessment. < 6 s indicates a maneuver that is too short, risking underestimation of FVC and FEV1.

VEXT [L] – Criterion for the correct start of exhalation. Extrapolated volume beyond the tangent on the flow-volume curve; according to ATS/ERS guidelines, used as a fluctuation criterion (exceeding 5% indicates too slow exhalation).

MEF25% [L/s] – Mid-expiratory flow at remaining 25% of FVC. End-of-expiration assessment. Highly dependent on the duration and completeness of exhalation; low value with premature termination of the maneuver.
FIVC [L] – Forced Inspiratory Vital Capacity. Assessment of the completeness of inspiration. A low value may indicate incomplete inspiration and affects the interpretation of the flow curve.
FIT100% [s] – Forced Inspiratory Time. Assessing inspiratory dynamics. Too long or too short a time suggests inadequate inhalation.

sec.p: Additionally, the average airflow velocity during forced inhalation (FVC/FIT100%) was calculated. Inspiration quality indicator. A low value indicates that inspiration is too slow, which distorts the shape of the flow-volume curve.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The significance of differences between the two independent groups (track athletes vs. cyclists) for all quantitative variables was assessed using appropriate statistical tests. The normality of data distribution was evaluated with the Shapiro-Wilk test, and the homogeneity of variances was assessed with Levene’s test. For data that met both the assumptions of normality and equal variances, Student’s t-test was applied. For data that violated the normality assumption, the non-parametric Mann-Whitney U test was used. All statistical tests were two-tailed, with a significance level set at p < 0.05. Given the sample size, the statistical power for detecting small-to-moderate effects was low. Therefore, primary emphasis is placed on the point estimates and confidence intervals of Cohen’s d to quantify the magnitude and precision of observed effects, rather than on binary significance testing. In addition to p-values, effect sizes were calculated to quantify the magnitude of the observed differences. For the t-test, Cohen’s d was used, and for the Mann-Whitney U test, the rank-biserial correlation was reported. Confidence intervals (95%) for Cohen’s d were calculated to quantify the precision of the effect size estimates.

To evaluate the reliability of the spirometry measurements, a test-retest analysis was performed. The following metrics were calculated for key spirometric parameters: the Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) using a two-way mixed-effects model for absolute agreement (ICC(2,1)), Pearson’s correlation coefficient (r), the Standard Error of Measurement (SEM), and the Minimal Detectable Change at the 95% confidence level (MDC95) [51]. ICC values were interpreted as follows: < 0.50 poor, 0.50–0.75 moderate, 0.75–0.90 good, and > 0.90 excellent reliability.

To identify independent determinants of key spirometric parameters, separate multiple linear regression analyses were performed for each athlete group (track and field, cycling). The following predictor variables were considered for inclusion in the models due to their potential physiological relevance: age, training experience, body height, body mass, and a set of body composition variables (Fat Mass, Fat-Free Mass). Due to the high number of predictors relative to the sample size and the presence of multicollinearity among body composition variables (assessed via Variance Inflation Factor, VIF), a parsimonious model-building approach was employed. A VIF value > 5.0 was considered indicative of substantial multicollinearity, warranting the exclusion of redundant variables from the models. This involved selecting a single, theoretically justified predictor from clusters of highly correlated variables (e.g., choosing Fat-Free Mass to represent the body composition dimension) to construct stable and interpretable final models with minimal multicollinearity. For each spirometric outcome (e.g., FEV1, FET100%, MEF25%), the final model was selected based on a combination of theoretical justification and statistical criteria, ensuring model stability and interpretability. The results of the regression analyses are presented as standardized beta coefficients (β) with their respective p-values. To complement the univariate comparisons, a multivariate analysis of similarity was performed to quantify the overall physiological dissimilarity between and within the athletic groups. Euclidean distances were calculated from the Z-standardized values of all anthropometric, body composition, and spirometric variables using the formula: d(x, y) = √[Σi (xi - yi)²], where xi and yi are the standardized values of the i-th variable for two athletes. A distance matrix was constructed for all participants. The mean within-group distance for each sport was computed as the average of all pairwise distances between individuals belonging to the same group. The between-group distance was calculated as the average distance between all possible pairs of athletes from the two different groups. This analysis provides a holistic measure of how distinct the overall physiological profiles of the two cohorts are.

Quantitative data are presented as means ± standard deviations (M ± SD) for normally distributed variables or as median and interquartile range (IQR) for non-normally distributed variables. All statistical analyses were conducted using Statistica software (TIBCO Software Inc., version 13.3, Palo Alto, CA, USA).

The analytical framework employed in this study, including the integration of multiple statistical approaches and sensitivity analyses, follows established principles for rigorous quantitative research in sports science [52].

sec.title: Participant characteristics, training history, and hearing loss
sec.p: The demographic characteristics, hearing levels, and training history of the participants are presented in Table 2. The road cycling cohort was significantly older than the track-and-field athletes (Cohen’s d = − 1.00 [95% CI: − 1.71, − 0.26]). The mean pure-tone average (PTA) indicated a profound level of hearing loss in both groups. Track-and-field athletes, however, presented with non-significantly higher (i.e., more severe) auditory thresholds in both ears compared to cyclists.

caption.p: Demographic characteristics, hearing levels, and training history of elite deaf athletes. Data are mean ± SD, with Cohen’s d and 95% CI for between-group comparisons

sec.p: Training patterns differed substantially between disciplines. Track athletes completed significantly more training sessions per week (Cohen’s d = + 0.79 [95% CI: 0.06, 1.52]), whereas cyclists engaged in longer daily training sessions (Cohen’s d = − 1.58 [95% CI: − 2.38, − 0.77]). No significant inter-group differences were found for total years of training experience or the number of Deaflympics participations.

sec.title: Somatic characteristics and body composition
sec.p: A comparison of somatic and body composition variables between the two athlete groups is presented in Table 3. The cyclists were significantly older than the track and field athletes (p < 0.05; Cohen’s d = − 1.00). For all other variables, no statistically significant differences were observed (p > 0.05). Consistent with these null findings, the effect sizes (Cohen’s d) for all body composition parameters were of small-to-moderate magnitude (|d| < 0.60). The 95% confidence intervals for these effect sizes are wide and routinely cross zero (e.g., for FAT%: d = − 0.45, 95% CI: − 1.19 to 0.25), reflecting substantial uncertainty. Therefore, although descriptive means suggest a trend toward higher adiposity in cyclists, the present data provide no reliable evidence for meaningful between-group differences in body composition.

caption.p: Comparison of somatic and body composition variables between elite male deaf track and field athletes and road cyclists. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) for each group. Between-group comparisons are expressed as Cohen’s d with 95% confidence intervals (CI)

table-wrap-foot.p: Somatic variables include: BH [cm] Body height, BM [kg] Body mass, BMI [kg/m²] Body mass index, BMR [kcal/day] Basal metabolic rate, FAT% [%] Body fat percentage; FAT [kg] Fat mass, FFM [kg] Fat-free mass, TBW [kg] Total body water, RL-FAT [kg] Right leg fat mass, RL-FFM [kg] Right leg fat-free mass, LL-FAT [kg] Left leg fat massM, LL-FFM [kg] Left leg fat-free mass, TRUNK FAT% [% Trunk fat percentage, TRUNK FAT [kg] Trunk fat mass, TRUNK FFM [kg] Trunk fat-free mass. No significant between-group differences (p > 0.05) were found for any somatic or body composition variable except for age (see text)

sec.title: Spirometric function assessment
sec.p: Spirometric parameters for both athlete groups are presented in Table 4. No statistically significant differences (p > 0.05) were observed for any variable between track-and-field athletes and road cyclists. In line with our effect size-focused approach, the observed Cohen’s d values indicated negligible to small between-group differences (|d| < 0.30) for most parameters, including FEF25–75%, MEF25%, VEXT, and FET100%. Descriptive trends suggested possible higher values in track and field athletes for FVC (d = + 0.41 [95% CI: − 0.29, 1.15]) and FEV1 (d = + 0.23 [95% CI: − 0.47, 0.96]), and in cyclists for PEF (d = − 0.25 [95% CI: − 0.95, 0.48]) and FIVC (d = + 0.46 [95% CI: − 0.27, 1.16]). The FEV1/FVC ratio approximated 80% in both groups. Given the wide confidence intervals and the acknowledged low statistical power, these trends should be interpreted with caution and do not support definitive conclusions about between-group differences.

caption.p: Comparison of spirometric variables between male track and field athletes and cyclists with hearing impairment. Values are mean ± SD. Effect sizes are presented as Cohen’s d with 95% CI

table-wrap-foot.p: Spirometric variables include: FVC [L] Forced Vital Capacity, FEV1 [L] Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 s, FEV1/FVC [%] the ratio of FEV1 to FVC expressed as a percentage, PEF [L/s] Peak Expiratory Flow, FEF25–75% Forced Expiratory Flow at 25–75% of FVC, FET100% [s] Forced Expiratory Time to 100% of FVC, VEXT [mL] Expiratory Reserve Volume, MEF25% Maximum Expiratory Flow at 25% of FVC, FIVC [L] Forced Inspiratory Vital Capacity, FIT100% [s] Inspiratory Time to 100% of FIVC. Percent-predicted values are not reported for PEF, FET100%, VEXT, MEF25% and FIT100% due to the lack of standardized reference equations for these parameters in athletic populations

sec.title: Bivariate correlation analyses
sec.p: Exploratory Pearson correlation analyses were performed to identify potential relationships between key variables. To control for the increased risk of Type I error, p-values should be interpreted with caution, and findings are considered hypothesis-generating for future studies.

sec.title: Track and field athletes
sec.p: The strongest correlations were observed between age and respiratory parameters. Age showed a strong positive correlation with FET100% (r = 0.83, p = 0.001) and moderate negative correlations with FEV₁ (r = − 0.59, p = 0.044) and VEXT (r = − 0.60, p = 0.037). Training experience was highly correlated with age (r = 0.96, p < 0.001).

sec.title: Cycling group
sec.p: In cyclists, FEV₁ was strongly correlated with body height (r = 0.78, p < 0.001). Moderate correlations were observed between FET100% and age (r = 0.52, p = 0.022), and between VEXT and fat mass (r = − 0.50, p = 0.029).

sec.title: Multivariate regression analyses
sec.p: Parsimonious multiple linear regression models were constructed to identify independent predictors of key spirometric parameters (Table 5). Due to the limited sample size and significant multicollinearity among body composition variables (e.g., VIF > 15 for FFM and Body Mass in initial models), a single, theoretically justified predictor (e.g., Fat-Free Mass) was selected from correlated clusters to ensure model stability. In all final models presented, Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) values were ≤ 1.0, indicating no residual multicollinearity.

caption.p: Predictors of spirometric parameters: Results of parsimonious multiple linear regression models for elite deaf athletes

table-wrap-foot.p: All models included age, training experience, body height, body mass, and body composition variables as potential predictors. † Beta coefficients > |1.0| indicate potential model instability due to multicollinearity and should be interpreted with caution. These models are presented for exploratory purposes and require validation in larger samples

sec.title: FEV1
sec.p: The determinants of FEV₁ differed fundamentally between sports. In track-and-field athletes, Fat-Free Mass (FFM) was the sole, dominant predictor, explaining a large proportion of variance (β = 0.95, Adjusted R² = 0.92, p = 0.002). In contrast, body height was the strongest predictor in cyclists (β = 0.71, Adjusted R² = 0.86, p < 0.001).

sec.title: FET100%
sec.p: Age was a significant positive predictor of forced expiration time in both groups (track: β = 1.12, Adjusted R² = 0.79, p = 0.045; cycling: β = 0.68, Adjusted R² = 0.41, p = 0.039).

sec.title: MEF25%
sec.p: A contrasting, sport-specific relationship was observed for maximal expiratory flow at low lung volumes. Body height was a significant negative predictor in track athletes (β = − 1.45, Adjusted R² = 0.85, p = 0.015) but a positive predictor in cyclists (β = 0.81, Adjusted R² = 0.58, p = 0.003).

sec.title: Interpretation of high beta coefficients
sec.p: Standardized beta coefficients with an absolute value exceeding 1.0 (e.g., for FFM and Age in track athletes) are theoretically possible but unusual, often indicating a model highly sensitive to the specific sample characteristics or remaining latent collinearity. These results should therefore be interpreted as indicating a very strong association within this cohort, but one whose precise magnitude may be unstable.

sec.title: Multivariate analysis of physiological profiles
sec.p: The multivariate Euclidean distance analysis quantitatively confirmed the distinct physiological profiles of the two athletic groups. The mean distance between the track-and-field and cycling cohorts (6.91) was substantially greater than the mean distances within each group (track: 5.84; cycling: 6.05), as detailed in Table 6.

caption.p: Multivariate analysis of physiological similarity: Within- and between-group Euclidean distances

table-wrap-foot.p: * Distances were calculated from a matrix of Z-standardized values for all anthropometric, body composition, and spirometric variables to eliminate scale differences

sec.p: This pattern demonstrates clear group separability. Furthermore, the slightly larger within-group distance for cyclists suggests a greater degree of physiological heterogeneity in this cohort compared to the more homogeneous track-and-field athletes.

sec.title: Reliability of spirometry measurements
sec.p: The test-retest reliability of spirometry parameters was assessed separately for track and field athletes (n = 12) and cyclists (n = 21). The results, including Intraclass Correlation Coefficients (ICC), Standard Error of Measurement (SEM), and Minimal Detectable Change (MDC95), are summarized separately for each group in Tables 7 and 8. Track and field athletes demonstrated exceptionally high reliability across all key spirometric parameters (Table 7). The primary outcomes, FVC and FEV1, showed near-perfect reliability, with ICC values of 1.00 and 0.99, respectively. The associated measurement error was minimal, with SEM values of 0.02 L for FVC and 0.04 L for FEV1. Consequently, the MDC95 was 0.06 L for FVC and 0.11 L for FEV1. Notably, parameters related to inspiratory maneuvers also exhibited excellent reliability, with ICCs of 0.98 for FIVC and 0.90 for FIT100%.

caption.p: Test-retest reliability of spirometry parameters in track and field athletes (n = 12)

table-wrap-foot.p: ICC Intraclass Correlation Coefficient, CI Confidence Interval, SEM Standard Error of Measurement, MDC95 Minimal Detectable Change at 95% confidence

caption.p: Test-retest reliability of spirometry parameters in cyclists (n = 21)

table-wrap-foot.p: ICC Intraclass Correlation Coefficient, CI Confidence Interval, SEM Standard Error of Measurement, MDC95 Minimal Detectable Change at 95% confidence

sec.p: The cycling cohort showed a different reliability profile (Table 8). While the core parameters FVC and FEV1 demonstrated excellent reliability (ICCs of 0.99 and 0.97), the measurement error was substantially larger. The SEM values were 0.06 L for FVC and 0.11 L for FEV1, resulting in MDC95 values of 0.17 L and 0.31 L, respectively. Several secondary parameters showed poor to moderate reliability. The forced inspiratory time (FIT100%) and the extrapolated volume (VEXT) were highly inconsistent, with ICCs of 0.17 and 0.29, respectively. The reliability of FIVC was good (ICC = 0.79), but its MDC95 was notably high at 1.22 L.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This pioneering study delivers the first comprehensive multivariate characterization of pulmonary function and body composition in elite deaf male athletes, comparing track-and-field and road cycling disciplines. By integrating rigorous spirometry (ATS/ERS-compliant) with multivariate Euclidean distance analysis and regression modeling, we demonstrate that: (1) elite deaf athletes exhibit distinct, sport-specific physiological profiles, and (2) the fundamental determinants of key ventilatory parameters differ fundamentally between disciplines. Crucially, a direct comparison with published data on elite hearing athletes reveals that these sport-specific adaptations largely mirror those seen in the hearing athletic population (Table 9). Therefore, the respiratory physiology of elite deaf athletes is defined not by auditory deficit, but by the characteristic adaptations of their sport.

caption.p: Comparison of somatic and spirometric characteristics of hearing athletes with deaf track and field athletes and cyclists

table-wrap-foot.p: Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Effect sizes are expressed as Cohen’s d for the difference between Hearing Athletes (HA) and Deaf Track & Field athletes (d-ATH) or Deaf Cyclists (d-CYCL)

FFM Fat-Free Mass, FAT % Body fat percentage, FVC Forced Vital Capacity, FEV1 Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 s, FEV1/FVC Ratio of FEV1 to FVC, PEF Peak Expiratory Flow, MEF25% Maximal Expiratory Flow at 25% of FVC

sec.title: Age of participants
sec.p: The significant age disparity between the cohorts, with cyclists being older than track and field athletes, aligns with established career longevity patterns in endurance sports. Road cycling is widely recognized as a discipline where peak performance can be maintained into later decades, attributable to the central role of aerobic capacity and experience, which attenuate the decline associated with age to a greater extent than in power-oriented disciplines. Our finding suggests that a similar pattern of extended competitive lifespan may exist among elite deaf athletes, highlighting that physiological determinants of career length might transcend hearing status.

sec.title: Hearing loss and training experience
sec.p: The uniformly profound hearing loss across our cohort confirms the homogeneity of the population qualifying for elite deaf sports. This degree of impairment entails significant communication barriers, which may explain why approximately one-third of athletes in both groups reported training exclusively in deaf-only environments. This finding raises important questions about potential barriers to integration and the availability of appropriate support in mainstream sports clubs - a topic that merits dedicated qualitative investigation.

The marked contrast in daily training volume, which was significantly greater in cyclists, directly reflects the divergent physiological demands of each discipline. The extended, steady-state efforts characteristic of road cycling necessitate a higher volume of low-intensity training to develop aerobic endurance–—a well-established principle in exercise physiology. In contrast, track-and-field training incorporates more varied elements, including power and technical work, which naturally leads to shorter daily sessions. The fact that this fundamental distinction is clearly evident in our data on deaf athletes reinforces that sport-specific training paradigms are applied universally, independent of auditory status.

sec.title: The qualitative assessment of spirometric results
sec.p: The qualitative assessment of spirometry revealed significant between-group differences in technical execution. Track-and-field athletes achieved a higher rate of “Excellent” maneuvers (83.3% vs. 66.7%) and a lower rate of “Barely Passing” efforts (8.3% vs. 23.8%) compared to cyclists, indicating superior consistency and proficiency.

Quantitative test-retest analysis robustly corroborated this finding and exposed its practical implications. While both groups showed ‘excellent’ reliability (ICC > 0.90) for primary outcomes like FVC and FEV1, the measurement precision differed drastically. The Minimal Detectable Change (MDC95) for FEV1 was nearly three times larger in cyclists (0.31 L vs. 0.11 L). Consequently, a physiologically meaningful change in FEV1 must exceed 0.31 L in a cyclist, compared to just 0.11 L in a track athlete.

This lower precision and higher technical variability in cyclists likely stems from greater cohort heterogeneity (wider age and experience range) and sport-specific physiology. The explosive efforts of track-and-field may better translate to the rapid, maximal exhalation required for spirometry than the sustained, submaximal breathing patterns dominant in cycling. This is reflected in the ‘poor’ reliability of technique-sensitive parameters like FIT100% and VEXT in cyclists (ICCs of 0.17 and 0.29), whereas track athletes maintained ‘excellent’ reliability (ICC = 0.90 for FIT100%).

Therefore, “Barely Passing” classifications in cyclists signify a cohort with diverse technical proficiency rather than uniformly lower pulmonary capacity. This underscores that spirometric data from heterogeneous athletic groups require population-specific thresholds for interpretation. Ensuring reliable assessment in such cohorts necessitates thorough coaching and ample practice trials to standardize maneuver execution.

sec.title: Neurophysiological adaptations in respiratory motor control
sec.p: A central and novel finding of this study is the consistent spirometric signature observed across both athletic cohorts: an abbreviated forced expiration (FET100% < 6 s) paired with an elevated extrapolated volume (VEXT). This pattern is physiologically distinct, as it coexists with preserved-and often superior-absolute lung volumes (FVC, FEV1), thereby dissociating it from parenchymal pathology.

We tentatively propose that the observed spirometric signature in deaf athletes may represent a fundamental neurophysiological adaptation in respiratory motor control, intrinsically linked to congenital deafness. In hearing individuals, the development of coordinated breathing is critically scaffolded by auditory feedback during vocalization and speech acquisition. The congenital absence of this sensory feedback loop may alter the neurodevelopmental trajectory of the respiratory control system, favoring a motor program that prioritizes rapid force generation over the sustained, finely modulated exhalation required for speech. The truncated expiration (short FET100%) and the explosive, less-ramped initiation of flow (elevated VEXT) are direct spirometric correlates of this adaptation.

This hypothesis is congruent with broader research documenting distinct adaptations in motor control among deaf individuals, particularly for behaviors typically guided by auditory cues, such as postural control and the temporal precision of gestures. Consequently, our findings provide a quantitative physiological window into how profound sensory loss reshapes fundamental motor systems via neuroplasticity.

This insight carries significant practical implications. It challenges the universal applicability of standard qualitative spirometry criteria-developed and validated in hearing populations-for assessing deaf individuals, suggesting a need for population-specific evaluation frameworks.

This novel hypothesis naturally directs key avenues for future research. First, it requires direct validation through neuroimaging (e.g., fMRI during respiratory tasks) and longitudinal studies in deaf populations with varying communication modalities (oral vs. sign language dominant). Second, while our study characterized airflow and volumes, a complete picture of ventilatory function and its trainability in elite deaf athletes will require incorporating dynamic measures of respiratory muscle strength (e.g., maximal inspiratory pressure, PImax) and endurance.

sec.title: Multivariate profiling and group distinctness
sec.p: Complementing the univariate comparisons, a multivariate analysis of Euclidean distances provided a holistic perspective on the overall physiological dissimilarity between the cohorts. The results confirmed that the global physiological profiles of track-and-field athletes and cyclists are quantitatively distinct. This finding, that sport-specific adaptation shapes a unique multivariate physiological signature, aligns with established patterns observed in hearing athletes, where endurance training, for instance, produces characteristic increases in lung volumes irrespective of other physiological variables [53, 55]. The mean Euclidean distance between the groups was markedly higher than the mean distances within each group. This pattern validates that sport-specific adaptation shapes a unique multivariate physiological signature in these athletes. The finding that the between-group distance was the largest of all comparisons provides robust evidence that the disciplines foster divergent physiological pathways, even at the elite level. Furthermore, the multivariate analysis revealed a subtle but important difference in the internal structure of each cohort. The mean Euclidean distance within the cycling group was higher than that within the track-and-field group. This indicates greater physiological heterogeneity among the cyclists. This finding aligns with the broader age range and greater disparity in training experience observed in the cycling cohort, as well as the more variable spirometric technique noted in our qualitative assessment. It suggests that the “phenotype” of an elite deaf track-and-field athlete may be more constrained and homogeneous, whereas the cycling cohort accommodates a wider spectrum of physiological characteristics, possibly reflecting diverse specializations (e.g., sprinters vs. climbers) or career stages within the sport.

sec.title: Comparative context: how deaf athletes measure against hearing peers
sec.p: To contextualize our findings, we compared the elite deaf athletes with published data on elite hearing athletes using standardized effect sizes (Cohen’s d; Table 9). Such cross-study comparisons require caution due to methodological heterogeneity in equipment, protocols, and population characteristics [39, 40, 53–55]; effect sizes here quantify magnitude, not permit direct statistical testing.

Within these constraints, the central finding is clear: the physiological profiles of elite deaf athletes are broadly comparable to those of their hearing counterparts, with observed differences aligning more closely with sport-specific adaptation than with auditory status.

The most consistent result was a lower body fat percentage in our deaf cohort compared to both hearing endurance athletes [53] (dATH ≥ + 1.09, dCYCL = + 0.61) and sedentary controls [39] (d ≈ + 1.2), underscoring their exceptional conditioning - a trait that transcends hearing ability.

For spirometric volumes (FVC, FEV1), comparisons yielded mixed directions (positive and negative Cohen’s d), reflecting high variability in the reference data itself. The most telling comparison involves road cycling. Despite being considerably older than a reference cohort of elite young hearing cyclists [40] (d = − 1.81), our deaf cyclists exhibited only marginally lower absolute lung volumes (d ≈ 0.7–0.8), demonstrating a highly competitive ventilatory capacity that aligns with established patterns of sport-specific adaptation [53].

In summary, the “deaf athlete” does not constitute a distinct physiological phenotype. These athletes are governed by the same principles of sport-specific adaptation as hearing athletes, with any variances more likely attributable to cohort demographics (e.g., age, training history) than to auditory deficit. This finding challenges preconceptions about the physical capacities of athletes with sensory impairments and affirms that the physiological determinants of elite performance operate across a diverse sensory spectrum.

sec.title: Synthesis of evidence relative to study aims
sec.p: This study aimed to address the significant gap in understanding the determinants of respiratory function in elite deaf athletes through a multidimensional analytical approach, testing two primary hypotheses.

sec.title: Sport-specificity of physiological profiles confirms H1
sec.p: Hypothesis 1 (H1) posited that track-and-field athletes would demonstrate higher spirometric values and more homogeneous ventilatory performance than road cyclists. The findings provide compelling, multi-faceted support for this hypothesis. While no statistically significant differences in individual spirometric parameters were found – a likely consequence of limited statistical power – definitive quantitative support emerged from the multivariate Euclidean distance analysis. This analysis confirmed distinct overall physiological profiles, with the mean distance between groups markedly exceeding the mean distances within each group. Crucially, the lower intra-group distance for the track-and-field cohort directly quantifies their greater physiological homogeneity, a finding aligned with their superior qualitative spirometric performance (83.3% "Excellent" vs. 66.7% in cyclists). This inherent homogeneity is further underscored by regression analyses, which revealed fundamentally different and more stable determinants of lung function (e.g., FEV1 predicted by fat-free mass in track athletes vs. body height in cyclists), confirming sport-specific physiological pathways.

sec.title: Comparable physiology to hearing athletes does not support H2
sec.p: Hypothesis 2 (H2) proposed that deaf athletes would present lower spirometric indices and less favourable body composition profiles compared with hearing endurance athletes. The results, contextualized via effect size comparisons (Table 9), do not support this hypothesis and, in fact, often suggest the opposite. Compared to hearing endurance athletes [53], our deaf track-and-field cohort demonstrated higher FVC and FEV1 (negative Cohen's d), indicating that high-level training can supersede any potential baseline deficit. However, compared to hearing handball players [54], the pattern was mixed: deaf athletes had higher FEV1 (d = -0.38) but lower FVC (d = +0.53). For deaf cyclists, the comparison with elite hearing cyclists [40] revealed higher spirometric values (FVC: d = +0.72; FEV1: d = +0.84) despite a considerable age difference (d = -1.81), underscoring their exceptional ventilatory capacity and suggesting that sport-specific adaptation outweighs any potential effects of auditory status.––Regarding body composition, the deaf athletes generally had lower body fat, aligning with an elite phenotype. The interpretation of these findings must be tempered by a critical methodological consideration. A post hoc power analysis revealed low statistical power and wide confidence intervals for the observed effect sizes [56]. Consequently, the non-significant results should be interpreted as indicative of considerable estimation uncertainty rather than definitive evidence of no difference.

sec.title: Overall interpretation and implications
sec.p: In conclusion, the physiological profile of the elite deaf athlete is not defined by limitation but by adaptation. The convergence of evidence – from traditional univariate comparisons, multivariate distance metrics, and regression analyses – firmly establishes that sport-specific demands are the primary drivers of both their absolute performance and the underlying physiological structure, effectively neutralizing hearing loss as a dominant factor in their ventilator physiology.

sec.title: Future research directions
sec.p: The novel findings of this study, particularly the distinctive spirometric patterns observed in deaf endurance athletes and their proposed neurological basis, delineate clear priorities for advancing research in this population. Addressing these priorities will require a coordinated, multi-faceted approach that moves beyond descriptive accounts toward mechanistic understanding and evidence-based practice.

Expanding cohort diversity and size through international collaboration represents the most pressing methodological priority. The primary limitation of the current study, as with much research on elite deaf athletes, is sample size. Structured international consortia are needed to pool data across nations and sports disciplines, representing the only feasible path to achieving the statistical power required for definitive subgroup analyses and the development of evidence-based guidelines tailored to this population [53].

Equally important is the need to elucidate the physiological and neurological mechanisms underlying the observed respiratory patterns. Future research must progress beyond description toward mechanistic understanding through integrative physiology incorporating measures of respiratory muscle strength, lung diffusion capacity, and maximal oxygen uptake. Neurophysiological investigations employing imaging techniques during respiratory tasks could directly test the hypothesis of altered central respiratory motor control resulting from congenital auditory deprivation, potentially explaining the distinctive flow-volume curve morphology reported in this and previous studies [46].

Longitudinal and comparative studies are essential to map the evolution of respiratory function and body composition throughout athletic development. Career-long tracking through multi-center designs would reveal how these parameters respond to sustained training loads and competitive demands [53]. Direct comparisons with hearing athletes, meticulously matched for discipline, age, and training load, remain essential for isolating the specific effects of hearing status from those attributable to athletic training alone.

The optimization of assessment and training methodologies warrants dedicated investigation. Research should examine how communication modalities, including visual cues and sign language interpretation, impact the understanding and technical execution of pulmonary function tests. Such work could inform protocols that minimize measurement error and enhance test validity in this population. Additionally, studies should design and test the efficacy of sport-specific respiratory training regimens, such as inspiratory muscle training, assessing their impact on both laboratory measures and athletic performance outcomes.

Finally, future studies should incorporate advanced respiratory and biomechanical analysis techniques. Optoelectronic plethysmography could provide detailed insights into chest wall kinematics during breathing maneuvers, while advanced measures of respiratory muscle function, such as the S-Index, would offer a more complete characterization of ventilatory capacity [57]. Integrating these approaches with the body composition and spirometric assessments established in the current study would progressively build a comprehensive understanding of the factors determining respiratory efficiency and athletic performance in deaf athletes.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p:
This study has several limitations that must be considered when interpreting the results.
Sample Size and Statistical Power. The small sample size (n=12 for track and field, n=21 for cycling), inherent to recruiting from the available population of elite deaf endurance athletes who met the strict inclusion criteria. A post hoc power analysis based on the observed effect sizes showed low statistical power (<0.30 for most variables) and wide 95% confidence intervals for Cohen's d [56]. In strict accordance with our stated analytical approach, we emphasize that non-significant results (p > 0.05) should be interpreted as a lack of reliable evidence for an effect, rather than definitive evidence of no effect. The observed effect sizes (Cohen's d) and their wide confidence intervals are the primary metrics for assessing the magnitude and uncertainty of between-group differences.
Methodological Constraints on Modeling. Severe multicollinearity among anthropometric predictors (VIF > 10 in initial models) limited our ability to construct complex, multivariate models. Therefore, regression coefficients with high absolute values (|β| > 1.0) should be interpreted as strong but potentially unstable associations specific to this sample, rather than as precise estimates of independent effects, and they require validation in larger samples.
Scope of Respiratory Assessment. The assessment of respiratory function was limited to standard spirometry and did not include dynamic measurements of respiratory muscle strength (e.g., maximal inspiratory pressure, PImax, or the S-Index) [57]. This prevents a complete characterization of ventilatory capacity and power reserves in these elite athletes. More importantly, the lack of this data means we cannot evaluate whether specific respiratory muscle training (RMT) has been utilized by the athletes or has contributed to their spirometric profile, which is a potential confounding factor when interpreting sport-specific adaptations (as noted in the Discussion). The questionnaire-derived data on training volume and frequency did not capture whether the athletes engaged in targeted inspiratory muscle training (IMT) or other breath-optimization practices. As RMT can significantly modify spirometric parameters and respiratory endurance, its uncontrolled and unmeasured effect represents a potential, unaccounted-for confounding factor that may have influenced the observed values and complicated direct inter-group comparisons.
Study Design and Generalizability. Finally, the cross-sectional design precludes any causal inference regarding the observed sport-specific adaptations. While we identified distinct determinants of lung function, we cannot rule out the influence of other unmeasured confounding factors, such as detailed training periodization or genetic predispositions. The generalizability of our findings is also constrained by the specific demographic of our cohort – elite adult male athletes. The morpho-functional relationships we described may not directly translate to female Deaf athletes, junior competitors, or those in other sports.

Despite these constraints, this study provides pioneering quantitative data and establishes a crucial foundation for future research, which should prioritize larger samples and a detailed characterization of RMT practices in this unique population.

sec.title: Conclussion
sec.p: This study provides the first integrated physiological characterization of elite Deaf athletes, revealing that their functional profile is predominantly shaped by sport-specific adaptation, overlaid with a unique respiratory neurology associated with congenital deafness.

Our multivariate analyses quantitatively confirmed that track-and-field athletes and road cyclists possess distinct physiological phenotypes. Track athletes demonstrated a power-oriented, homogeneous profile with superior technical proficiency in spirometry, evidenced by near-perfect test-retest reliability (ICC > 0.90) and low measurement error (MDC₉₅ for FEV1 = 0.11 L). In contrast, cyclists exhibited an endurance-adapted, more heterogeneous physiology, reflected in greater within-group variability and larger measurement errors (MDC95 for FEV1 = 0.31 L).

The most distinctive finding was a consistent spirometric signature across both cohorts: abbreviated forced expiration (FET100% < 6s) coupled with elevated extrapolated volume (VEXT). We interpret this not as pulmonary dysfunction, but as a manifestation of altered respiratory motor control – a fundamental neurophysiological adaptation arising from the congenital absence of auditory feedback for speech-breath coordination.

Comparative analysis indicates that elite Deaf athletes achieve a physiological status comparable to, and in aspects such as body composition often superior to, their hearing counterparts. This demonstrates that systematic, high-level training can produce a high-performance physiology independent of auditory capability.

These findings suggest the need for a revised approach in practice: coaches and clinicians are recommended to move beyond a deficit-based view and adopt discipline-specific frameworks for assessing and training Deaf athletes. Tailored interventions, such as visual-guided breathing coaching and extended familiarization with testing maneuvers, are essential to optimize assessment precision, particularly in endurance athletes.

Future research must prioritize elucidating the neurophysiological mechanisms underlying the observed respiratory signature and establish comprehensive, longitudinal monitoring using the population-specific reliability metrics defined in this study.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was approved by the Bioethics Committee of the Collegium Medicum, Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń, Poland (Approval No. KB 236/2023). Written informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to enrollment. For athletes younger than 18 years, consent was additionally obtained from their legal guardians and coaches. All participants or their guardians were fully informed about the study procedures before providing written consent. The research was conducted in accordance with the ethical principles of the Declaration of Helsinki.
